summary: This PR adds fine-grained checks (scheme, hostname, and port) to `rebuild_auth` so that the `Authorization` header is stripped on any cross-origin redirect, not just host changes. The core security fix for https→http scheme downgrades is sound, but there are correctness issues around implicit default-port normalisation, a potential gap if session-level `auth` re-applies credentials after the strip, and notable gaps in test coverage for the new port-change branch.

findings.title: Session-level `auth` may re-add Authorization header after it is stripped, defeating the fix
findings.detail:
The `rebuild_auth` method deletes `headers['Authorization']` and then calls `prepared_request.prepare_auth(...)` which, if `session.auth` is set, will re-attach an `Authorization` header to the prepared request — including to a downgraded `http://` destination. This means the credential-leak fix is only effective when auth was supplied as a per-request header rather than via `session.auth`.

Audit `resolve_redirects` to confirm whether `self.auth` is passed into `prepare_auth` after the strip. If so, the session-level `auth` callable/tuple must also be suppressed (or cleared) when a cross-origin redirect is detected, or the credential-protection logic must be moved further down the call chain so that `prepare_auth` is aware of the origin change.

Note: confidence is moderate (0.5 from the original finding) because this depends on the exact control flow of `resolve_redirects`; it should be verified by tracing the full redirect loop, but the risk is significant enough to flag as critical pending that audit.

findings.title: No test exercises the new port-change stripping branch
findings.detail: The `or original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch introduced on line 246 is not covered by any existing or new test. `test_auth_is_stripped_on_redirect_off_host` redirects to a different hostname (not a different port on the same host), and `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` covers only the scheme branch. A test that redirects from e.g. `http://host:8080/a` to `http://host:9090/b` (or `http://host:80/b`) is required to confirm this branch works correctly and to guard against regressions.

findings.title: Port comparison does not normalise implicit default ports
findings.detail:
When a URL omits a port (e.g. `https://example.com/`), `urlparse(...).port` returns `None`, not the scheme's default (443 for HTTPS, 80 for HTTP). This causes two problems:

1. **False positive (over-stripping):** A redirect from `https://example.com:443/a` to `https://example.com/b` evaluates `443 != None → True` and strips `Authorization` even though the canonical origin is identical — a functional regression for servers that spell out the default port explicitly.
2. **False negative risk (future):** Any future reordering that short-circuits before the scheme check could fail to catch a downgrade if ports are both implicit.

Fix: normalise each port before comparing — substitute the scheme default when `port is None` (80 for http, 443 for https) — or compare canonical origin tuples after normalisation:

```python
def _default_port(scheme):
    return {'http': 80, 'https': 443}.get(scheme)

orig_port = original_parsed.port or _default_port(original_parsed.scheme)
redir_port = redirect_parsed.port or _default_port(redirect_parsed.scheme)
if (original_parsed.scheme != redirect_parsed.scheme or
        original_parsed.hostname != redirect_parsed.hostname or
        orig_port != redir_port):
    del headers['Authorization']
```

This also makes the intent (comparing canonical root URL components) more self-documenting, as suggested by the style reviewer.

findings.title: Scheme-change stripping covered only by an integration test; no fast offline test
findings.detail: `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` depends on live `httpbin` and `httpbin_secure` fixtures, so it may be skipped in CI environments without TLS support. Add a fast, offline unit/mock-level test using `SessionRedirectMixin` or the existing `RedirectSession` test helper to verify that `Authorization` is stripped when the scheme changes from `https` to `http`, ensuring the critical security branch is always exercised regardless of network fixture availability.

findings.title: No regression test confirming Authorization is RETAINED on same-origin path-only redirects
findings.detail: There is no test that verifies `Authorization` is **not** stripped when scheme, hostname, and port are all unchanged but only the path differs. `test_auth_is_retained_for_redirect_on_host` covers a simple same-host redirect but does not explicitly exercise the boundary of the new three-part condition, leaving open the possibility that a future change inadvertently broadens the stripping logic without a failing test to catch it.

findings.title: Port comparison fails to account for implicit default ports
findings.detail:
When a URL omits a port (e.g. `https://example.com/`), `urlparse(...).port` returns `None`, not the default port for that scheme (443 for HTTPS, 80 for HTTP). This means a redirect from `https://example.com:443/path` to `https://example.com/path` (or vice versa) will be treated as a cross-origin redirect and strip the `Authorization` header unnecessarily — a regression for valid same-origin redirects that spell out the default port explicitly.

More critically in the other direction: two URLs that differ only in scheme but whose default ports for their respective schemes happen to be omitted will still be caught by the scheme check, so the core security fix is sound. However, the port check is still incorrect: `original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` is `None != None` → `False` when both omit the port, which is correct, but `None != 443` → `True` (stripping auth) when one omits and the other spells out the HTTPS default port — that's a false positive.

Consider normalising each port value: if it is `None`, substitute the default port for the associated scheme (80 for http, 443 for https) before comparing, or use a helper such as `urllib3.util.url.parse_url` which normalises default ports.

findings.title: Auth not stripped on scheme downgrade (https → http) due to port comparison short-circuit
findings.detail:
The new condition uses `or` across three sub-checks, which is correct in isolation, but the specific security concern from issue #4716 — an https→http redirect — is only caught by the scheme check. However, the fix is sound for that case.

A subtler issue: the condition does NOT handle the case where a redirect goes to a URL with an explicit port that is the default for its scheme (e.g., https://example.com:443/... vs https://example.com/...). `urlparse` leaves `port` as `None` when not specified, and as `443` when explicit — so `original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` would evaluate `None != 443` as `True` and incorrectly strip the Authorization header even though the canonical root URL hasn't changed. This is a security-adjacent correctness issue (over-stripping is low-risk) but the converse — if logic were accidentally inverted — could be a leak. More critically: if a server redirects from `https://host/a` to `https://host:443/b`, the auth header is stripped even though the destination is identical. This is a functional regression but not a direct credential leak.

More importantly for security: verify that the scheme check reliably covers https→http. `urlparse('https://...').scheme` returns `'https'` and `urlparse('http://...').scheme` returns `'http'`, so `original_parsed.scheme != redirect_parsed.scheme` correctly catches the downgrade. This part of the fix is correct and closes the reported vulnerability.

findings.title: https→http redirect still leaks Authorization header if port differs but scheme does not check first (ordering is fine, but audit the no-auth-header path)
findings.detail:
Looking at the broader flow: the `rebuild_auth` method only strips the Authorization header from `headers` (the PreparedRequest's headers dict). However, if a `auth` callable/tuple was attached to the session, `rebuild_auth` re-applies auth credentials via `self.rebuild_auth` after stripping. This means even after stripping the Authorization header, the session-level `auth` may re-add it via `prepare_auth` for any host including a downgraded http host.

Specifically, after the `del headers['Authorization']` the code calls `self.rebuild_auth(prepared_request, response)` from `resolve_redirects`, which itself calls `prepared_request.prepare_auth(self.merge_environment_settings(...).get('auth') or self.auth, ...)`. If `self.auth` is set, it will re-attach the Authorization header to ALL redirect destinations regardless of scheme — meaning the https→http credential leak is NOT fully fixed if auth was configured at the session level rather than per-request.

This should be audited by checking whether `resolve_redirects` re-applies `session.auth` after the header strip.

findings.title: Multi-condition `if` could be simplified by comparing canonical root URL strings
findings.detail:
The existing code collapsed the host-change check into a single comparison. The new patch expands it into three separate OR'd conditions. Elsewhere in the codebase the parsed URL is already available, so an alternative that is both terser and more self-documenting is to compare the two "canonical root URLs" directly:

```python
if (original_parsed.scheme, original_parsed.hostname, original_parsed.port) != \
        (redirect_parsed.scheme, redirect_parsed.hostname, redirect_parsed.port):
    del headers['Authorization']
```

This also makes the intent conveyed by the PR description (

findings.title: Port-change redirect stripping not covered by any test
findings.detail: The new `or original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch (line 246) is not exercised by any test. `test_auth_is_stripped_on_redirect_off_host` redirects to a different hostname (not a different port on the same host), and `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` covers the scheme branch. There is no test that redirects from e.g. http://host:8080/... to http://host:80/... (or the equivalent) to confirm that Authorization is stripped when only the port changes.

findings.title: No unit-level / mock test for scheme-change stripping — only an integration test
findings.detail: `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` (lines 1580-1588) is an integration test that requires both `httpbin` and `httpbin_secure` fixtures. There is no fast, offline (unit/mock-level) test using `SessionRedirectMixin` / `RedirectSession` that verifies the Authorization header is stripped when the scheme changes (https→http). This means CI environments without TLS httpbin support may leave this branch uncovered.

findings.title: No test for auth being RETAINED when only path changes (same scheme+host+port)
findings.detail: The refactored condition now uses three sub-expressions. There is no test confirming that Authorization is NOT stripped when the scheme, hostname, and port are all identical but the path is different — i.e. a regression guard that the guard condition isn't accidentally too broad. `test_auth_is_retained_for_redirect_on_host` only covers a simple same-host redirect but does not explicitly exercise the boundary of the new three-part condition.

This means the credential-leak fix is only effective when auth was supplied as a per-request header rather than via `session.auth`.

If so, the session-level `auth` callable/tuple must also be suppressed (or cleared) when a cross-origin redirect is detected, or the credential-protection logic must be moved further down the call chain so that `prepare_auth` is aware of the origin change.
